content.full_text:
Fern PolrrrcAt- PnecrrcEs CoularssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95g14-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
October 29,2002
Jean B. Savaree, City Attorney
City of Belmont - Office of the City Attorney
Post Office Box 1065
San Carlos, CA 94070
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-268
DearMs. Savaree
Re
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Parks and Recreation
Commissioner George {ranen regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the political
Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTION
May Parks and Recreation Commissioner George Kranen participate in the discussion
and vote concerning the renovation of a park that is located 550 feet from his personal residence?
CONCLUSION
Yes, Mr. Kranen may participate in the disbussion and vote conceming the renovation as
long as it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateialirnancial effect on
his residential property.
FACTS
You request advice on behalf of George Kranen, Parks and Recreation Commissioner for
the City of Belmont. The question relates to Mr. Kranen's participation in discussions regarding
renovation of certain parks within the City of Belmont. The parks and recreation commission
sits as an advisory body. It makes recommendations to the city council on matters relating to
parks and recreational programs and facilities.
' Govero-"rrt Code sections 8 1000 - 9l}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections l g l09-
18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
:::


File No. A-02-268
Page No. 2
The parks and recreation commission is currently looking at proposed improvements for
a number of parks within the city. One of those parks is locatedippioximately 550 feet from
Mr. Kranen's personal residence. The proposed improvements to tie park consist of a children,s
playground, Ptrk bench, fencing, grading, retaining walls and guardraiils. The estimated costs of
these improvements range from $50,000 to $75,000. The parks and recreation commission is
being asked to review these proposed improvements and make a recopmendation to the city
council whether or not it should authoize installation of these improvements at the park.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by theiiown financial interests. (Section
81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from .,making,,,
"patticipating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to .,influence,, a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The Commission has
adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding yhether an official has a disqualifting
conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700 O)(t) - (8).),
Step One: Is Mr. Kranen apublic official?
Under the Act, a "public official" is defined, in part, as a "member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." (Section 82048.) A..local govemment
agency" means a county, city or district of any kind, including a school districi, or any other
local political subdivision or any county board or commission. (Sect ion 82041.) As a member
of the City of Belmont Parks and Recreation Commission, Mr. Iir*", is apublic official and
subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.3 (See e.g., McMahin Adviceletter, No,
A-02-1s0.)
govemmental decision?
If Mr. Kranen were to vote on this matter, he would be "making a governmental
decision-" (Regulation 18702.1.) Likewise, if he were to make *y."Iorrirendations or
2 Please note that in December of 2000, the Commission adopted regulations that revised the conflict-of-
interest eight-step analysis. Under the revised regulations, because Mi. Kranen's property is not within 500 feet of
the decision, nor the subject of the decision, his property is considered indirectly fuoI".; in the decision and the
financial effect of the decision is presumed notto bematerial. (Regulations 187i04.2, nlOi.ZlAl.) A copy of these
regulations, as well as a copy of our pamphlet, "Canl Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overyiew,, ur" "r"lor"j for your
consideration.
3 Also note that if the parks and recreation commission only makes recommendations to the city council,
and those recommendations are not merely'?ubber-stamped" by the city council as final decisions, then the parks
and recreation commission may be a purely advisory committeJ not sudect to the conflict-of-interest provisions of
the Act. Regulation 18701 and the Traverso Advice Letter, No. I-01-124 have been included for your information.
If you believe that this commission qualifies for this exception, please write in with more inforrnation for a
determination on that question.
,,


File No. A-02-268
Page No. 3
participate in discussions involving the park project, he would be "participating in making a
governmental decision. " (Regulat ion I 87 02.2.)
step Three: Does the public official have an economic interest?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising from
economic interests. There are six kinds of such economic interests:
1. A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section Sl t0Z(a);regulation
18703.1(a));
2. A public official has an economic interest in which he or she is a director, fficer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (section SiiOf la;;
regulation I 8703. 1 (b));
3. A public official has an economic interest in real property inwhich he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section SZf df (b); regulation 1g703.2);
4. A public official has an economic interest in any source of income,including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prioito the
decision. This includes income to any business entity or trust when the public official
owns a 10-percent interest or greater and his or her pro rata share is valued at $500 or
more (section 87103(c); regulation 1g703.3);
5. A public official has an economic interest in any source of siftsto him or her if the
gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to t[e decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 B.g;
6. A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate famili. This is known as
the "personal financial effects" rule (section g7103, regulation rsio:.s).
You have inquired about only one economic interest, Mr. Kranen,s personal
residence, so this analysis will be limited to that real property interest. Forihe purposes
of this letter it will be assumed that Mr. Kranen has at least a $2,000 investment in his
personal residence and, therefore, has an economic interest in his real property.
s
the decision?
The next step is determining whether the economic interests will be involved
directly or indirectly in the decision. (Regulation 18700(bX4).) When apublic official
has an economic interest in real property it is directly invoived'in a goverirmental
decision if it "is the subject of the governmental decision, or if any p-art of that real


File No. A-02-268
Page No. 4
property is located within 500 feet... of the govemmental decision.,' (Regulation
18704.2(a).) You have indicated that Mr. Kranen's property is 550 r"it tom the property
in question
After determining that Mr. Kranen's property is not directly involved using the
500-foot distance test, we next determine ifhis prop;rry is directly involved blcause it
qualifies as the "subject of the govemmental decisitn.'i4 Thesit*ations listed as
examples of what is the'osubject" of a governmental decision are in regulatio n 1g704.2.
Examples include zgn:flrlg or rezoning, the issuance, denial or revocation of a license, the
imposition or repeal of a tax or fee, and similar types of sifuations.
According to your facts, the proposed improvements to the park consist of the
additions of a "children's playground, park bench, fencing, grading, retaining walls and
guardrails." These improvements do not fall into any of tlie categories listed, nor do they
appear to be of the type considered when defining,,subject of thJgovemmental
decision." Therefore, Mr. Kranen's property doe-s not appearto be the,.subject of the
govemmental decision" and is not directly affected by the decision.
Once Mr- Kranen identifies his relevant economic interests, he must evaluate
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a;t"ririfi;cial effect
on any of those economic interests by ascertaining the applicable materi;lity standard
(regulations 1s700(b)(5), 18705 - 1 8705.5) and flien aeteimining whether ii is reasonably
foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulations 18700@t(6), 1g706.) An effect
of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there is a subst antiallikelihood ttrat it witt
occur. Certainty is not required, but the effect must be more than a mere possibility.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development comm. (lgg7) 1g6 cal.App.3d gg3,
989; witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 car.App.3dgl1, g22; HarperAdvice Letter, No. 4_96_
2e8.)
Since Mr. Kranen's property is indirectly involved, we apply the materiality
standards of regulation 1 8705.2(b)(1 ):
"Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial
effect of a governmental decision on real property which is
indirectly involved in the govemmental dicisionls
presumed not to be material. This presumption may be
rebuffed by proof that there are specific ciicumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect,
and the nature of the real property in which the public
official has an economic interest, which make ifreasonably
a Note that if the property is not within 500 feet, then the economic interest is indirectly involved
and the materiality standard of regulation 18705.2(b) for real property will be applied in the next step. The
effect could still be material and reasonably foreseeable.


File No. A-02-268
Page No. 5
foreseeable that the decision will have amateialfinancial
effect on the real property in which the public official has
an interest. Examples of specific circumstances that will be
- 
considered include, but are not limited to, circumstances
where the decision affects:
(A) The development potential or income producing
potential of the real property in which the official has an
economic interest;
(B) The use of the rear property in which the officiar has
an economic interest;
(c) The character of the neighborhood incruding, but not
limited to, substantial effects on: traffic, view, piiacy,
intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or similar traits
of the neighborhood.,,
After finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the siandarA wfu be met. Regulation lg706
states that a "material financial effect on an economic interest is reaso-nably foreseeable,
within the meaning of Govemment Code section 87l}3,if it is substantialiy likely that 
-
one or more of the materiality standards [citation] applicable to that "roro*i" interest
will be met as a result of the govemmental decisi6n.'i In this instance, the amount of
effect on Mr. Kranen's property does not appear to meet the threshold amount necessary
for finding a"matenal financial effect." However, ifupon evaluation, Mr. Kranen
determines that this threshold has been reached, then he has a conflict of interest with
respect to this park project. If not, then under the facts provided, Mr. Kranen does not
hau.2a conflict of interest in the park renovation decisio-n as it reiates to his personal
resrdence--
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Galena West
Counsel, Legal Division
Enclosures
cwje

5 we have omitted^steps seven and eight, which are exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules,
since Mr' Kranen has been found not to have aconflict of interest. Please refer to the enclosed pamphlet,
"can I vote? conflicts of Interest overyiew" for further information.

May Parks and Recreation Commissioner George Kranen participate in the discussion
and vote concerning the renovation of a park that is located 550 feet from his personal residence?

Yes, Mr. Kranen may participate in the disbussion and vote conceming the renovation as
long as it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateialirnancial effect on
his residential property.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Parks and Recreation Commissioner George Kranen participate in the discussion
and vote concerning the renovation of a park that is located 550 feet from his personal residence?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Mr. Kranen may participate in the disbussion and vote conceming the renovation as
long as it is not reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have amateialirnancial effect on
his residential property.
